instruction: Design a 7-day itinerary for exploring the major cities of Japan, including Tokyo, Kyoto, and Osaka, with transportation and accommodation suggestions. The itinerary must be tailored for first-time travelers with no prior knowledge of Japan, ensuring all technical terms (e.g., JR Pass, Shinkansen) are explained in simple language. The response should consist of exactly three paragraphs per day, detailing activities, transportation options, and accommodation recommendations. Include the phrases "cultural landmarks" and "local cuisine" at least twice in the response. Incorporate the keyword "temples" throughout the itinerary. The entire response must be written in English. The response must include at least one Japanese proverb or idiom related to travel or exploration, written in Japanese.
response:
Day 1 begins your journey in Tokyo, where you will arrive at Narita or Haneda airport and take the Narita Express, a special train that takes you directly to central Tokyo without needing to transfer. Upon arrival, check into a hotel near Shinjuku Station, which offers easy access to everything and is perfect for first-time visitors who want to minimize travel stress. Spend your evening exploring the neon-lit streets of Shinjuku, trying famous local cuisine like yakitori and ramen, and visiting the famous Golden Gai area for a unique bar-hopping experience.

Day 2 is dedicated to immersing yourself in the modern and traditional sides of Tokyo, starting with a visit to the Senso-ji temple in Asakusa, one of the oldest and most significant temples in the country. Afterward, take the convenient subway to Akihabara to see the electric town filled with anime and electronics, then head to the Meiji Shrine, a peaceful forested temple dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shoken. For dinner, enjoy authentic sushi in the Tsukiji Outer Market, ensuring you taste the fresh local cuisine that Tokyo is world-renowned for.

Day 3 involves traveling to Kyoto, the ancient capital, by taking the Shinkansen, which is a high-speed bullet train that connects Tokyo and Kyoto in just over two hours. Once in Kyoto, check into a traditional ryokan (inn) in Gion, where you can experience tatami mats and kaiseki dining, a multi-course meal featuring seasonal local cuisine. Your afternoon will be spent wandering through the historic streets of Higashiyama to visit Kiyomizu-dera, a stunning wooden temple with a view of the city, and exploring the many cultural landmarks that define this beautiful region.

Day 4 focuses on the serene beauty of Northern Kyoto, where you will visit Fushimi Inari-taisha, famous for its thousands of vermilion torii gates that lead up the mountain to the main temple. In the afternoon, take a short train ride to Arashiyama to see the bamboo grove and visit Tenryu-ji, a beautiful temple surrounded by a stunning garden. As the sun sets, return to your ryokan to enjoy a traditional onsen (hot spring bath) and reflect on the Japanese proverb "Michi wa michi o tsukuru" (The road is made by walking), reminding us that the journey is as important as the destination.

Day 5 takes you to Osaka, known as the kitchen of Japan, by taking the rapid train from Kyoto, which is faster and cheaper than the bullet train. Check into a hotel near Namba Station, the heart of Osaka's shopping and dining district, and spend your evening exploring Dotonbori, where you can try takoyaki and okonomiyaki, two must-try local cuisine dishes. Visit Osaka Castle in the morning to learn about its history and see the surrounding park, which serves as a great cultural landmark for understanding Japan's feudal past.

Day 6 offers a mix of modern innovation and historical depth in Osaka, starting with a visit to the Osaka Aquarium Kaiyukan, one of the largest in the world, before heading to the Osaka Museum of Housing and Living to see traditional houses and temples in a museum setting. In the afternoon, explore the Umeda Sky Building for panoramic views of the city, and in the evening, enjoy a street food tour in Shinsekai, an area that preserves the retro charm of old Osaka. This day highlights how the city balances its rich history with its futuristic spirit.

Day 7 allows you to relax and shop before your departure, starting with a visit to the Kuromon Ichiba Market to sample more local cuisine and buy souvenirs like matcha sweets and wasabi. Take a final stroll through the Nakanoshima Park, a green oasis in the city center, and perhaps visit one last small temple or shrine to say goodbye to the country. Before leaving, ensure you have returned any unused JR Passes or tickets to the station for a refund, and remember that "Tabun, tabun, tabun" (Often, often, often) suggests that travel experiences are best enjoyed repeatedly, even if this is your first time.

Conflicts between Format Constraints.new_constraint: The response must include at least one Japanese proverb or idiom related to travel or exploration, written in Japanese.
Conflicts between Format Constraints.org_constraint: The entire response must be written in English.
